summary: Gruber analyzes the 2009 rumors about Apple talking to Verizon about the iPhone and a potential tablet device. He argues that while it makes strategic sense for Apple to eventually expand beyond AT&T exclusivity, Tim Cook's specific comments about CDMA being a dead-end technology suggest a Verizon iPhone isn't imminent. He draws an extended parallel between Apple's iPod strategy and its likely iPhone strategy, predicting Apple will eventually release a smaller, cheaper 'iPhone Lite' alongside the flagship model. The core argument is that Apple learned from its Mac-era mistake of prioritizing profits over market share, and will aggressively expand the iPhone platform the same way it expanded iPods.
key_points: AT&T activates only 42% of iPhones sold worldwide, making the U.S. carrier relationship less critical to Apple than the U.S. tech press assumes
Tim Cook's specific dismissal of CDMA as a dying technology signals no near-term Verizon iPhone, but Apple's leaked flirtation with Verizon keeps pressure on AT&T
Apple's iPod strategy — expanding from one product to a family with cheaper, smaller variants — is the template for the iPhone's future
Steve Jobs explicitly identified Apple's late-1980s mistake of choosing profits over market share as what allowed Windows 95 to destroy the Mac's monopoly
A potential Apple tablet could use Verizon's EVDO network without contradicting Cook's statements about CDMA and the iPhone
money_quotes: AT&T isn't Apple's iPhone partner. They're just Apple's iPhone partner in the U.S.

I'd find it surprising if they didn't at least talk to Verizon just to hear an offer, and perhaps more importantly, to leak the flirtation to the press so as to keep the pressure on AT&T to offer Apple the best possible terms.
That's just not something Cook would say if Apple were planning to announce a CDMA phone in June — and without a CDMA iPhone, there's no iPhone for Verizon.
The jackass contingent is going to blame Apple for not releasing it rather than blame the rumor reporters for being wrong.
At the critical juncture in the late '80s, when they should have gone for market share, they went for profits. They made obscene profits for several years. And their products became mediocre.
When it comes to managing the balance between per-unit profit and overall market share, Apple is determined to err on the side of market share.

A typical company that found itself selling millions of $400 hard-drive-based digital music players would try its best to continue selling the same $400 hard-drive-based digital music players for as long as it could.
key_insight: Apple's iPod playbook of aggressively expanding into cheaper form factors and broader distribution will inevitably be applied to the iPhone, because Jobs learned from the Mac's market share disaster that prioritizing profits over growth is how monopolies die.
